skill.name: github-multi-repo
skill.description: Coordinate GitHub work across multiple repositories with dependency sync, architecture templates, release workflows, and swarm-style task planning.
skill.summary: Coordinate multi-repository GitHub changes, dependency alignment, and architecture workflows.
skill.author: Claude Flow Team

security_audit.summary: Static analysis correctly identified many shell, GitHub CLI, filesystem, network, and environment-variable patterns in SKILL.md. Review found these patterns are mostly documented automation examples for a multi-repository GitHub workflow, not confirmed malicious behavior, but they can mutate many repositories and should run only with explicit user approval and scoped credentials.

medium_findings.title: Broad Multi-Repository Command Execution
medium_findings.description: The skill documents shell workflows that list repositories, clone them into temporary directories, run package-manager commands, create branches, commit changes, push to remotes, and open pull requests. This is expected for the skill, but it can change many repositories if executed without careful scoping.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The static command-execution findings are real because the examples invoke Bash with gh, git, npm, jq, and shell loops. Confidence is high that the behavior is intentional automation rather than hidden malware.
medium_findings.recommendation: Require explicit repository allowlists, dry runs, and user approval before executing commands that commit, push, create pull requests, or modify dependencies.

medium_findings.title: GitHub API Writes and Hidden Configuration Paths
medium_findings.description: Examples use GitHub API and MCP calls to update repository files, write workflow files under .github, and push generated template files. These actions are legitimate for repository automation but can alter CI and project configuration across repositories.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The locations show write operations to package.json, .claude paths, and .github workflow paths. The examples use placeholder repositories and documented workflows, so malicious intent is not confirmed.
medium_findings.recommendation: Limit GitHub tokens to the target organization and review proposed file changes before applying them.

medium_findings.title: External Webhook Secret Configuration
medium_findings.description: The communication example configures an external webhook URL and reads process.env.WEBHOOK_SECRET. This may be safe when the endpoint is controlled by the user, but it combines network coordination with a secret value.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The endpoint is an example.com placeholder and no direct exfiltration call is shown. The combination of a webhook URL and environment secret still requires review before real deployment.
medium_findings.recommendation: Document that webhook URLs must be user-controlled and that secrets must never be logged, stored in prompts, or sent to untrusted endpoints.

low_findings.title: Weak-Crypto Static Matches Are Documentation False Positives
low_findings.description: Several weak-cryptography static matches appear to come from ordinary words, file names, architecture labels, or Git SHA usage rather than cryptographic implementation. No evidence found of MD5, SHA-1 password hashing, or custom cryptography in the reviewed context.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Reviewed locations show Git reference SHA retrieval, synchronization labels, and related command names. These do not implement weak cryptographic algorithms.
low_findings.recommendation: No security remediation is required for these static matches, but scanner rules should distinguish Git SHA references from cryptographic use.

low_findings.title: Temporary Directory Usage in Examples
low_findings.description: The skill uses /tmp paths for cloned repositories, repository lists, and audit output. This is common for examples, but real runs should avoid predictable shared paths when handling private code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The filesystem locations are concrete and repeated. The risk is limited because they are examples and not a packaged executable script.
low_findings.recommendation: Use unique temporary directories with restrictive permissions and clean them up after each run.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Command Substitution Inside GitHub API Calls
dangerous_patterns.description: The examples embed shell command substitutions such as reading branch SHAs, base64-encoding files, and generating branch names. These patterns are useful but can become injection risks if user-controlled values are interpolated.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Command substitution is directly visible in the cited snippets. The examples do not show untrusted raw input, so the finding is a pattern risk rather than confirmed exploitation.
dangerous_patterns.recommendation: Quote variables, validate repository names, and prefer structured API calls over shell interpolation when possible.

dangerous_patterns.title: Automated Dependency Mutation Across Repository Sets
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes workflows that run npm update, npm install, npm audit fix, tests, commits, pushes, and pull request creation across discovered repositories. This can introduce supply-chain or operational risk if dependency changes are not reviewed.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited snippets explicitly perform dependency mutation and repository write operations. The intent matches the skill purpose, but the operational blast radius is broad.
dangerous_patterns.recommendation: Run dependency workflows in dry-run mode first, pin package registries, and require human review before merging generated pull requests.

content.value_statement: Teams often struggle to keep dependencies, documentation, and repository patterns aligned across many GitHub projects. This skill provides structured prompts and workflows for coordinated multi-repository analysis, updates, testing, and pull request planning.
actual_capabilities: Plans coordinated work across multiple GitHub repositories and service groups.
Provides examples for discovering repositories with GitHub CLI and filtering metadata.
Outlines dependency alignment, documentation sync, and integration test workflows.
Describes architecture template creation and standard repository structure patterns.
Shows release, dashboard, health check, and dependency graph command patterns.
Uses swarm-style task roles for coordinators, analysts, testers, and documenters.
limitations: It is a documentation skill, not a standalone audited automation binary.
Examples require GitHub CLI, repository permissions, and careful token scoping.
Broad repository writes can be risky without dry runs and human review.
Webhook and coordination examples need user-controlled infrastructure before use.

use_cases.title: Synchronize Microservice Dependencies
use_cases.description: Coordinate dependency updates, tests, and pull requests across related service repositories.

use_cases.title: Standardize Repository Architecture
use_cases.description: Plan shared templates, workflow files, and documentation structures for many teams.

use_cases.title: Plan Cross-Repository Releases
use_cases.description: Map package relationships, check integration status, and organize release tasks across projects.

prompt_templates.title: Discover Related Repositories
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the github-multi-repo skill to plan a read-only inventory for these repositories: [repo list]. Identify languages, package files, and dependency links. Do not modify files.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Dependency Sync
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the github-multi-repo skill to create a dependency synchronization plan for [package name] across [repository list]. Include test order, risk checks, and pull request strategy.

prompt_templates.title: Standardize Repository Templates
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the github-multi-repo skill to compare repository structures across [organization or repo list]. Propose standard workflow, documentation, and template updates for review.

prompt_templates.title: Coordinate a Cross-Repo Security Patch
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the github-multi-repo skill to design a dry-run first security patch workflow for [affected package]. Require explicit approval before branches, commits, pushes, or pull requests.

output_examples.input: Inventory frontend, backend, and shared library repositories before a dependency update.
output_examples.output: A repository map that lists each project, package manager, key dependencies, test command, ownership notes, and recommended update order.

output_examples.input: Create a plan to update a shared library used by six services.
Standardize GitHub Actions workflows across service repositories.
output_examples.output: A proposed workflow template, list of repositories needing changes, validation steps, and review gates before any file updates.

best_practices: Start with read-only discovery before allowing commits, pushes, or pull request creation.
Use scoped GitHub tokens and explicit repository allowlists for every workflow.
Review generated dependency and workflow changes before merging any pull request.
anti_patterns: Running organization-wide write operations without a dry run or repository allowlist.
Using broad personal access tokens for automated multi-repository changes.
Sending webhook secrets or repository data to endpoints that the team does not control.

faq.question: Does this skill directly modify repositories?
faq.answer: The skill contains examples that can guide repository changes. Users should require approval before executing write operations.

faq.question: Which tools does it expect?
faq.answer: The examples reference GitHub CLI, npm, jq, git, Claude Flow, and MCP-style GitHub operations.

faq.question: Can it work with private repositories?
faq.answer: Yes, if the user has valid access. Use scoped credentials and avoid broad organization permissions.

faq.question: Is it safe for production repositories?
faq.answer: It can be used safely with read-only discovery, dry runs, review gates, and limited credentials.

faq.question: Can it coordinate dependency updates?
faq.answer: Yes. It provides patterns for finding consumers, updating packages, running tests, and preparing pull requests.

faq.question: Does it include deployment automation?
faq.answer: It focuses on coordination, synchronization, and repository workflows. Deployment steps need project-specific review.
